title: On democratic conventions in the year of 2016 - marathoner - Quora

content.content:

Interpersonal Skills Trai...

Caucuses
Democracy in the United S...
Bernie Sanders
Activism
+ 5
Angli Liu
 
PhD student in CS at University of Washington10y
On democratic conventions in the year of 2016

I've had the fortune to have attended the 2016 democratic conventions on the precinct and district level. It has been a great learning experience. There is no better way for a foreigner to see how democracy works in the U.S. and further have a sense of participation than this.

On the precinct level, I chaired the district-43 precinct-1278 caucus meeting on March 26th at Hamilton International Middle School, helping dozens of people voice their support towards either Bernie Sanders or Hillary Clinton. And I collected the votes from my precinct, aggregating the numbers and presenting the result over to the next level. I was glad to be able to participate as non-citizen Chair because we prepared a lot on this, including my background material printouts stating my purpose and experiences used to run for Chair, and polishing the skill of "working the room". Plus, it was great to be able to know some great people there who encouraged me to carry this on. Here is a photo of me announcing the procedure of entire event to the voters from my precinct.

The legislative district level convention was held on April 17th at Cascadia Elementary. This event was huge. The school hosted delegates in the 43 Legislative District elected from each precinct, which is proportional to the number of people who voted in the last election season. In this convention, my goal is to get enough signatures from participants to support me to get an official status as a non-voting observational shadow delegate in the convention on the next level, and also present another resolution on advancing the GIX program. I successfully collected 50 signatures, one of which is from a candidate running for 43rd Legislative District Representative. With the signatures and the well-prepared resolution paper, we managed to hand it in to the committee, who is going to discuss which resolutions to pass. Again, I learned a lot about "working the room" -- I had to practice what I had to say to convince people over and over again. The feedback people gave me as I practiced helped me figure out what's important to say for this matter. I've also learned to not to take rejections personally in such activities as people have the freedom. It turned out 32 signatures were collected very quickly with a surprisingly high 98% success rate. See the picture below.

Below is a picture taken at the convention.

These experiences are truly rewarding, in terms of interpersonal skills and being an activist. Looking forward, my goal is to go the national democratic convention in July (which means I'll need to get support from the conventions on the county level, state level, etc.), observe how democracy in U.S. works in every detail, and present more resolutions for good.

Thanks to my friends for offloading a lot of the workload, and a great deal of planning. I was able to enjoy the fun of it and learn more from it only because of them.

title: A pint of Arabic Coffee at Cedars of Lebanon Restaurant - marathoner - Quora

content.content:
Food and Cuisine
University District (Seat...
The Cedars
Arabic Coffee
Lebanese Food
+ 1
Angli Liu
 
PhD student in CS at University of Washington9y
A pint of Arabic Coffee at Cedars of Lebanon Restaurant

The restaurant sequesters itself just a little bit from the bustling University Way. The food this restaurant serves, of all the middle eastern restaurants around the University District, is outstanding. And the staff is welcoming, giving the restaurant a family feel.

Quite unique among all the foods is the Arabic coffee. The coffee has a bitter and alcohol flavor to it. Quite thick, too. After I finished the coffee, the owner was so generous and refilled the cup. I asked what was in the coffee that contributed to the alcohol flavor. She said it was Cardamom, with a big grin. I said it was the first time I tried Arabic Coffee. And I’ll definitely come back, have another cup of Arabic Coffee and dig into more Lebanese food next time.

Another castle charted on my food and culture map!
